title: Daily Briefing — Saturday, June 6
tldr: Anthropic published a coordinated-pause proposal on June 4 (“When AI builds itself”) — the kicker: Claude now authors >80% of code contributions to its own codebase, and Anthropic engineers absorb ~8× more merged code daily than two years ago. Altman's response: “build the bomb, then sell people the shelter.” The IPO-track frontier lab just publicly raised the recursive-self-improvement alarm.

The bipartisan “Great American AI Act” discussion draft dropped (June 4) — Obernolte (R-CA) + Trahan (D-MA), 269 pages. 3-year preemption of state laws that regulate model DEVELOPMENT (deployment laws are NOT preempted), mandatory safety frameworks + semi-annual third-party audits + incident reports for labs >$500M revenue, codifies Commerce's CAISI. This directly invalidates last week's “plan around state laws” advice.

OpenAI shipped ChatGPT Dreaming V3 (June 4) — the background memory-synthesis pattern we taught two weeks ago (Anthropic's “dreaming,” Harvey's reported 6× task-completion lift) is now the default architecture in ChatGPT, with a ~5× compute cut that enables Free-tier rollout next.

MiniMax M3 (June 1, weights still pending) — claimed SWE-Bench Pro 59, 1M-token context via a new sparse-attention variant (MSA), priced at ~5–10% of GPT-5.5. A second Chinese open-weight lab landing in frontier-class tier — the price-pressure thread is now a tier, not a model.

Capital is quietly rerouting out of LLM-only bets. Generalist AI raised $400M at $2B (Nvidia, Bezos, Fei-Fei Li) for robot foundation models; Flourish raised $500M at $2.5B (Bezos ~$50M, Lux, GV) chasing the brain's ~20-watt energy budget via connectomics. Embodied AI + neuromorphic efficiency are where the next “frontier” money is going.

blocks.sub: Anthropic calls for a coordinated pause — and accidentally reveals where it actually is
blocks.p: On June 4 the Anthropic Institute (Jack Clark + Marina Favaro) published “When AI builds itself” arguing for a coordinated, multi-lab, multi-country slowdown if frontier labs approach “full recursive self-improvement.” The hard datapoint inside the post: Claude now writes >80% of code contributions to its own codebase, and Anthropic engineers are merging roughly 8× more code per day than two years ago. Sam Altman dismissed the post as marketing — “build the bomb then sell people a shelter.”
blocks.why: Three days after filing an S-1 at a reported ~$47B run-rate, the frontier lab most likely to IPO first publicly declared we are in striking distance of self-improving AI. That is not a safety-team blog post — it is a signal to investors, regulators, and competitors simultaneously. The implicit boast (“our model writes most of our model”) is also the strongest public claim yet that AI is now meaningfully accelerating AI R&D — exactly the substrate Trump's June 2 EO and the new Obernolte–Trahan draft (below) are racing to govern.
blocks.doIt: Run the same measurement on yourself. What % of your team's merged code is AI-authored this month? What % of design docs? What % of test cases? Track the trend monthly — it is the single best leading indicator of when your hiring plan and quality-gate process need rewriting. Teams that don't measure this will over-hire by ~6 months and ship more bugs.

blocks.sub: Great American AI Act flips Wednesday's policy thesis: federal ceiling on state DEV laws
blocks.p: Reps Jay Obernolte (R-CA) and Lori Trahan (D-MA) released a 269-page discussion draft on June 4. Three moves matter: (1) 3-year preemption of state laws specifically regulating the development of frontier AI models — but use and deployment laws are NOT preempted; (2) frontier developers above $500M annual revenue must publish safety frameworks, file critical-incident reports, and submit to semi-annual third-party audits; (3) codifies the Commerce Department's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) as the federal locus.
blocks.why: Last week's weekly told you to “plan around state laws — the federal picture is unsettled.” One week later, Congress drafted a federal ceiling that would freeze California's frontier transparency act and Colorado's AI Act on the development side. Pair this with the Trump EO: the Executive Branch gets the covered-model early-access lever, Congress gets the safety-framework + audit lever, and states are told to wait. Even as a draft, this is the most coherent US federal AI regime to date — and it is genuinely bipartisan, which means it is not a posture bill.
blocks.doIt: Two roadmap changes. (1) If you're under $500M revenue, the audit/incident requirements don't bind you — but your enterprise buyers will ask within 90 days. Build the artifact once (model card + eval suite + incident runbook + red-team summary) and emit jurisdiction-specific exports for EU AI Act + Trump EO + CAISI. (2) Watch the “development” vs “deployment” line carefully — your fine-tuning pipeline may sit on the preempted dev side, while your customer product stays under state law. That is a new internal compliance boundary to draw on the org chart this quarter.

blocks.sub: ChatGPT Dreaming V3: the technique we taught two weeks ago is now the default
blocks.p: On June 4 OpenAI rolled out Dreaming V3 to ChatGPT Plus/Pro (US first, Free tier next). A background process synthesizes a hierarchical memory state from your conversations continuously, replacing the old “saved memories” list as the primary store. The two key numbers: ~5× less compute to serve memory features (which is why Free can get it), plus a visible “memory summary” page for inspection and editing.
blocks.why: Two weeks ago the May 30 weekly taught this concept under its original name (Anthropic's “dreaming,” May 6) and flagged Harvey's reported 6× task-completion lift after enabling it. It is now the default architecture in both leading consumer chat products. Session-scoped chat is the legacy paradigm; persistent, asynchronously-curated memory is the new floor. Two implications: any user-facing AI product without persistent memory just lost a leg of competitive parity — the gap will widen weekly. And the compute economics flipped: 5× cheaper means memory becomes free-tier table stakes, not a Pro upsell — so any pricing model that monetizes memory directly is on borrowed time.
blocks.doIt: Build it into your own agent this week — no special infra required. See today's Sharpen for a drop-in template. The Harvey 6× is mostly an artifact of the pattern, not the provider.

blocks.sub: MiniMax M3: open-weight, 1M-context, frontier-class — at ~5–10% of GPT-5.5 pricing
blocks.p: MiniMax launched M3 on June 1 (weights + technical report scheduled within ~10 days; treat the benchmark claims as directional until independent reproduction, ~June 11). Claims: SWE-Bench Pro 59 (ahead of GPT-5.5 and Gemini 3.1 Pro, just behind Opus 4.7), 1M-token context, native multimodality, OpenRouter pricing at roughly 5–10% of frontier proprietary tokens. The architecture novelty is MiniMax Sparse Attention (MSA) — attention computed only over selected segments, dodging quadratic scaling.
blocks.why: Saturday's price-spread thread (DeepSeek 100× cheaper output) just got reinforced by a second Chinese open-weight lab landing in frontier-class tier. The pressure is no longer one model — it is a tier. And 1M context as a commodity kills a lot of RAG complexity for medium-sized codebases and document sets: if your retrieval pipeline exists primarily to compress context, MSA-style sparse attention is the architectural shift that eats it.
blocks.doIt: When the M3 weights ship (~June 11), run your hardest long-context eval against M3 vs DeepSeek V4-Pro vs Opus 4.8 on three real tasks: (1) refactor across a ~500K-token monorepo, (2) synthesize from a 200-page contract bundle, (3) review a 50-file PR. The winner becomes your default for that task class — model selection per task class, not per app, is now the right granularity.

blocks.sub: Where the capital actually went this week: embodied AI + 20-watt brains
blocks.p: Two of this week's biggest non-frontier-lab rounds were not LLM bets. Generalist AI: $400M at $2B (Radical led; Nvidia NVentures, Bezos Expeditions, Fei-Fei Li, Naval, Eric Yuan in) for embodied foundation models — GEN-0 (Nov '25) reportedly “brought robots into the pretraining era,” GEN-1 (April '26) added dexterous manipulation. Flourish: $500M at $2.5B (Bezos ~$50M, Lux, GV, Catalio; cofounded by Internet Explorer creator and CTRL-labs founder Thomas Reardon) building Cortex AI aimed at the brain's ~20-watt energy budget versus the ~600W+ of a single training chip, via connectomics.
blocks.why: Last week's weekly named compute + power as the moat. This week's funding placed opposite bets on that thesis: (a) move AI out of the data center into the physical world (Generalist) so revenue scales with deployed units, not GPUs; (b) re-architect the substrate so frontier capability stops needing the data center at all (Flourish). Either bet, if it pays, breaks the “whoever has the biggest cluster wins” narrative. The fact that the same investor (Bezos) is on both rounds tells you the smart money treats them as complementary hedges against frontier-lab compute lock-in — not competing theses.
blocks.doIt: Add a column to your model-router spreadsheet for the next 12 months labelled “physical-world / embodied”. Even if you're software-only today, Bedrock AgentCore and OpenAI's ACP will start exposing physical-action APIs by 2027 — having a mental map of who's building the robot-side stack is how you don't get blindsided when an agent in your product needs to dispatch a real-world job.

blocks.sub: Build “dreaming” into your agent this week — before everyone has it
blocks.p: OpenAI and Anthropic just normalized the pattern. Here is the minimum viable version you can ship in a day, no special infra. After each session, fire a distillation prompt against the transcript and persist a JSON blob (Postgres / Redis / S3 — your call). On the next session, inject the blob instead of the raw transcript. That is the entire technique.
list: The distillation prompt: You are curating long-term memory for an AI assistant. From the conversation below, output STRICT JSON with these keys: stable_preferences (user habits, tools, formatting, no-go topics — only if reaffirmed or repeated), current_projects (name + 1-line status + next step + due date if mentioned), open_questions (asked but not resolved), recurring_mistakes (errors we made — and the corrected behavior to remember), drop_list (anything from prior memory the user contradicted or marked stale). Rules: do not invent. If a field has no evidence, return []. Quote the conversation span that justifies each entry. Be terse.
Inject the blob, not the transcript at the start of the next session — you get continuity without burning the token budget.

Merge, do not overwrite. Each cycle, ask the model to merge the new blob into the prior blob, applying the drop_list first. This curation loop is what produced the Harvey 6× — the storage layer alone won't.

Ship a “memory summary” view in your UI. OpenAI ships it; your competitors will. Users who can see and edit their AI's memory trust it more, and your support volume drops because they fix wrong context themselves.

The trap to avoid: never auto-merge sensitive fields (names, employers, health, finances) without a user-correction loop. Treat memory edits like database migrations — reviewable, reversible, audit-logged. This is exactly the surface area that will get audited under the Great American AI Act's incident-reporting rule for any lab over $500M.

sources: Anthropic Institute (“When AI builds itself,” Jun 4 — Jack Clark + Marina Favaro) · Al Jazeera / Quartz / Business Standard / Crypto Briefing (Anthropic pause proposal; Altman “bomb / shelter” response) · Roll Call / Axios / Nextgov / Public Citizen / ITI / Bloomberg Law (Great American AI Act discussion draft, Obernolte–Trahan, Jun 4) · OpenAI / Windows News / gHacks / TechTimes (ChatGPT Dreaming V3 rollout, ~5× compute cut, memory summary page) · VentureBeat / The Decoder / Kingy AI / Codersera (MiniMax M3; SWE-Bench Pro 59 claim; 1M context; MSA attention) · The Robot Report / SiliconANGLE / Bloomberg / Bezos Expeditions (Generalist AI $400M at $2B) · SiliconANGLE / TechFundingNews / The Next Web (Flourish $500M at $2.5B; Cortex AI; ~20-watt brain target) · Crunchbase News (Jun 5 weekly funding roundup). Benchmark, valuation, and run-rate figures are directional until independently verified.
